title: Federated Healthcare Learning Framework — Compliance Report

controls.description: Implement technical policies to allow access only to authorized persons
controls.implementation: Each hospital node only accesses its own local patient data. The central server never receives or stores raw patient records. Data access is enforced by the federated architecture itself.
controls.evidence: HospitalClient class encapsulates data; FederatedServer has no data access methods

controls.description: Implement mechanisms to record and examine system activity
controls.implementation: All federated training rounds, model updates, privacy budget consumption, and aggregation events are logged with timestamps. SecureAggregator maintains an integrity verification audit trail.

controls.description: Protect ePHI from improper alteration or destruction
controls.implementation: HMAC-SHA256 signatures verify the integrity of all model updates during federated aggregation. Tampered updates are detected and rejected.

controls.description: Verify the identity of persons seeking access to ePHI
controls.implementation: Hospital clients are identified by unique IDs and HMAC keys. In production, this would be enhanced with TLS mutual authentication and certificate-based identity verification.
controls.evidence: Client IDs embedded in HMAC signatures

controls.implementation: Model updates (not raw data) are transmitted with HMAC integrity verification. Differential privacy adds noise to prevent inference from updates. Production deployment would add TLS 1.3 encryption for all communications.

controls.description: ePHI is de-identified to reduce privacy risk
controls.implementation: Differential privacy provides mathematical de-identification: the published model is (ε,δ)-differentially private, meaning individual patient records cannot be reconstructed from model parameters. Raw data never leaves hospital premises.
controls.evidence: Gaussian mechanism with configurable ε and δ parameters

controls.description: Personal data shall be adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary
controls.implementation: Only model gradients/weights are transmitted between nodes. No raw patient data is ever collected, copied, or centralized. The federated architecture inherently enforces data minimization.

controls.description: Data collected for specified, explicit purposes only
controls.implementation: The model is trained solely for disease outbreak prediction. Training configuration explicitly defines the learning objective. Model cannot be repurposed without reconfiguration.

controls.description: Data kept only as long as necessary for the purpose
controls.implementation: Raw patient data remains at source hospitals and follows their retention policies. Model updates are ephemeral — used for aggregation then discarded. Only the final aggregated model is retained.

controls.description: Implement appropriate technical measures to protect data
controls.implementation: Privacy is architecturally enforced: (1) federated learning prevents data centralization, (2) differential privacy provides mathematical guarantees against inference, (3) gradient clipping bounds individual contribution, (4) HMAC ensures update integrity.

controls.description: The right to have personal data erased
controls.implementation: A hospital can withdraw from the federation at any time. The global model can be retrained without their updates. Full "machine unlearning" would require retraining from scratch, which is supported but computationally expensive.

controls.description: Assessment of impact on data protection for high-risk processing
controls.implementation: The privacy-accuracy trade-off analysis serves as a quantitative DPIA. Membership inference attack simulation measures actual data leakage risk. The compliance report documents all privacy controls and their effectiveness.

controls.description: Appropriate safeguards for cross-border data transfers
controls.implementation: No patient data crosses borders. Only differentially private model updates (mathematical abstractions, not personal data) are exchanged. This satisfies data sovereignty requirements by design.

overall_assessment.recommendation: The framework provides strong privacy guarantees through federated learning and differential privacy. For production deployment, add TLS 1.3, certificate-based authentication, and integrate with institutional identity providers.
